1.description: (Color online) Service Time and Prices by Retailer and Competition Level Notes. (a) Mean service time by competition level. (b) Mean basket price by competition level. (a) Average service time for each grocer against the number of active online grocers in each local market. (b) Average basket price for each grocer against the number of active online grocers in each local market. Both graphs are based on monthly data from August 2016 to July 2019. (a) A clear pattern of downward-sloping curves of service time, where service time is considerably shorter in markets served by more online grocers. For instance, Shufersal’s (the largest grocer’s) mean service time in monopolistic markets is 43 hours. In markets with five online grocers, its mean service time is 20 hours. (b) Grocers choose different price levels, but the price levels (including promotions) are identical across markets characterized by different levels of competition. Finally, looking across the two panels, we observe that pricier online grocers tend to offer shorter service times.

2.description: (Color online) Incumbent’s Service Time and Prices by Competition and Demand Levels Notes. (a) Mean service time by demand and competition. (b) Basket price on low- and high-demand days. (a) Average service time of Shufersal (the incumbent) by the number of online grocers in the local market, separately for low- and high-demand days, based on monthly data from August 2016 to July 2019. (b) Daily price of a basket of 52 products sold on Shufersal’s online channel, separately for Sunday and Thursday for each week from August 2016 to July 2019. (a) Service time on high-demand days is longer than on low-demand days. Service time is shorter and the difference between high- and low-demand days is smaller in more competitive markets. (b) Unlike service time, Shufersal’s prices (including promotions) do not vary with demand conditions over the days of the week. That is, there is no discernible difference between the price of the basket on low-demand days (Sundays) and on high-demand days (Thursdays). Similar patterns for the other online grocers are shown in Figure C.5 in Online Appendix C.

3.description: (Color online) Changes in Market Structure and Online Grocers’ Expansion Notes. (a) Market structure evolution. (b) Online grocers’ expansion. (a) Number of markets served by a different number of online grocers in each month during the sample period. In August 2016, 73 markets were monopolies, and in 25 markets at least four online retailers were active. In July 2019, the last month in the sample, 44 local markets were monopolies, and in 42 markets at least four online retailers were active. (b) Number of markets served by each online grocer in each month of the sample period. We exclude Shufersal because it operated in all 172 markets throughout the sample period. Victory, Yenot Bitan, and Rami Levy grew, respectively, from 21, 38, and 40 markets in August 2016 to 48, 74, and 86 markets in July 2019. Mega, which faced considerable financial difficulties during the period, exited many of the local markets it served.

4.description: (Color online) Incumbent’s Service Time Before/After Entry by Pre-Entry Competition and Demand Levels Notes. (a) Low-demand day. (b) High-demand day. The figure plots the average of Shufersal’s service times in the 120 markets that experienced entry before and after a rival grocer entered the market. The figure distinguishes between low-demand (a) and high-demand (b) days and between different pre-entry competition levels. Service times are shorter on low-demand days than on high-demand days. Also, service times are shorter in more competitive markets on both low- and high-demand days. We observe a decrease in service times that occurs before entry, and this decrease is more pronounced on low-demand days and in monopolistic markets.

5.description: (Color online) Effect of Entry on Service Time by Competition and Demand Levels (Reference Period: More Than 6 Months Before Entry) Notes. (a) The effect of entry into monopolistic markets. (b) The effect of entry into duopolistic markets. (c) The effect of entry into competitive markets. The figure plots the coefficients of 𝛽 𝑘 β k for k ranging from −6 to 6 and their 95% confidence intervals obtained from estimating Equation (1) , with a full set of relative indicator variables for 𝑘 ≥ − 6 k ≥ − 6 , using different subsamples. The reference period is set to more than six months before entry. Standard errors are clustered at the market level. The dependent variable is the incumbent’s log service time in the local market on Saturday night (a) or on Wednesday night (b). (1) Estimated effects of entry into monopolistic markets on the incumbent’s service time where all markets that did not experience entry are used as the control group. (2) Estimated effects of entry into duopolistic markets on the incumbent’s service time where the control group is markets that did not experience entry and have at least two active online grocers. (3) Estimated effects of entry into competitive markets (with three or four active online grocers), and the control group is markets that did not experience entry and have at least three active online grocers. All specifications include market fixed effects and month–year fixed effects. The results suggest that the incumbent shortens its service time when it faces entry into monopolistic markets. On low-demand days this change in service time begins shortly before entry.

6.description: (Color online) Effect of Entry on Service Time by Competition and Demand Levels (Reference Period: More Than 12 Months Before Entry Notes. (a) The effect of entry into monopolistic markets. (b) The effect of entry into duopolistic markets. (c) The effect of entry into competitive markets. The figure plots the coefficients of 𝛽 𝑘 β k for k ranging from −12 to 12 and their 95% confidence intervals obtained from estimating Equation (1) , with a full set of relative indicator variables for 𝑘 ≥ − 1 2 k ≥ − 12 , using different subsamples. The reference period is set to more than 12 months before entry. Standard errors are clustered at the market level. The dependent variable is the incumbent’s log service time in the local market on Saturday night (a) or on Wednesday night (b). (1) Estimated effects of entry into monopolistic markets on the incumbent’s service time where all markets that did not experience entry are used as the control group. (2) Estimated effects of entry into duopolistic markets on the incumbent’s service time where the control group is markets that did not experience entry and have at least two active online grocers. (3) Estimated effects of entry into competitive markets (with three or four active online grocers), and the control group is markets that did not experience entry and have at least three active online grocers. All specifications include market fixed effects and month–year fixed effects. The results suggest that the incumbent reduces service time when it faces entry in monopolistic markets, and that on low-demand days the reduction begins shortly before entry.
